title: “‘How, monks, should a monk contemplate so that his consciousness is not distracted or scattered externally, and does not abide internally, and is not agitated…
content: “‘How, monks, should a monk contemplate so that his consciousness is not distracted or scattered externally, and does not abide internally, and is not agitated by clinging? If his consciousness is not distracted or scattered externally, does not abide internally, and is not agitated by clinging, then there is no arising, origination of suffering regarding future birth, aging, and death.’ Good sirs, how is consciousness distracted and scattered externally? Here, good sirs, when a monk sees a form with the eye, consciousness pursues the sign of form, is tied to the enjoyment of the sign of form, is bound by the fetter of the enjoyment of the sign of form—this is called consciousness distracted and scattered externally. When he hears a sound with the ear … smells an odor with the nose … tastes a flavor with the tongue … feels a touch with the body … cognizes a phenomenon with the mind, consciousness pursues the sign of phenomenon, is tied to the enjoyment of the sign of phenomenon, is bound by the fetter of the enjoyment of the sign of phenomenon—this is called consciousness distracted and scattered externally. Thus, good sirs, is consciousness distracted and scattered externally.”

title: “Monks, what are the further things that should be done? ‘We must guard the sense faculties. When the eye sees a form, do not grasp…
content: “Monks, what are the further things that should be done? ‘We must guard the sense faculties. When the eye sees a form, do not grasp the general feature, do not grasp the particular feature. Those causes whereby, if the eye faculty were not restrained, covetousness, sorrow, evil, unwholesome states would arise—we will restrain that cause, guard the eye, practice restraint of the eye. When the ear hears a sound … nose smells an odor … tongue tastes a flavor … body feels a touch … mind cognizes phenomena, do not grasp the general feature, do not grasp the particular feature. Those causes whereby, if the mind faculty were not restrained, covetousness, sorrow, evil, unwholesome states would arise—we will restrain that cause, guard the mind, practice restraint of the mind.’”

title: “Monks, what is non-clinging and non-worry? Here, monks, a well-taught noble disciple regards form as: ‘This is not mine, this I am not, this is…
content: “Monks, what is non-clinging and non-worry? Here, monks, a well-taught noble disciple regards form as: ‘This is not mine, this I am not, this is not my self.’ For him, that form changes and alters. When form changes and alters, no sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, or despair arise in him. He regards feeling … perception … formations … consciousness as: ‘This is not mine, this I am not, this is not my self.’ For him, that consciousness changes and alters. When consciousness changes and alters, no sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, or despair arise in him. Thus, monks, is non-clinging and non-worry.”

title: “Monks, devas and humans delight in form, are excited by form. When form changes, fades, and ceases, monks, devas and humans dwell in suffering. They…
content: “Monks, devas and humans delight in form, are excited by form. When form changes, fades, and ceases, monks, devas and humans dwell in suffering. They delight in sound … in odor … in taste … in touch … Monks, devas and humans delight in phenomena, are excited by phenomena. When phenomena change, fade, and cease, monks, devas and humans dwell in suffering. Monks, the Tathāgata, the Arahant, the Perfectly Enlightened One, having directly known as they actually are the origination, the cessation, the gratification, the peril, and the escape in regard to form, does not delight in form, does not cherish form, is not excited by form. When form changes, fades, and ceases, monks, the Tathāgata dwells at ease. He does not delight in sound … in odor … in taste … in touch … Having directly known as they actually are the origination, the cessation, the gratification, the peril, and the escape in regard to phenomena, the Tathāgata does not delight in phenomena, does not cherish them, is not excited by them. When phenomena change, fade, and cease, the Tathāgata dwells at ease.”

title: Fear, suffering, and disease, A boil, and a dart, Bondage and mud, And also a womb — These are called sensual pleasures, Where worldlings become…
content:
Fear, suffering, and disease,
A boil, and a dart,
Bondage and mud,
And also a womb —
These are called sensual pleasures,
Where worldlings become attached,
Sinking into the beautiful,
They enter a womb again.
When a monk is ardent,
Not lax, mindful,
He crosses this dangerous path,
This path so hard to cross.
He sees beings living in fear,
Because of birth and aging,
Oppressed and conquered.

title: “Monks, what is the burden? The five aggregates of clinging are the answer. What five? The form aggregate of clinging, the feeling aggregate of clinging,…
content: “Monks, what is the burden? The five aggregates of clinging are the answer. What five? The form aggregate of clinging, the feeling aggregate of clinging, the perception aggregate of clinging, the formation aggregate of clinging, the consciousness aggregate of clinging. This, monks, is called the burden. And what, monks, is the bearer of the burden? The person — the venerable one of such a name and such a clan. This, monks, is called the bearer of the burden. And what, monks, is the taking up of the burden? It is this craving that leads to renewed existence, accompanied by delight and lust, delighting here and there — that is, craving for sensual pleasures, craving for existence, craving for non-existence. This, monks, is called the taking up of the burden. And what, monks, is the laying down of the burden? It is the remainderless fading away and cessation of that same craving, the abandoning and relinquishing of it, freedom from it, non-reliance. This, monks, is called the laying down of the burden.”

title: “This city is built of bones, Plastered with flesh and blood; Within dwells aging and death, Conceit and hypocrisy.” “Even the king’s splendid chariot wears…
content:
“This city is built of bones,
Plastered with flesh and blood;
Within dwells aging and death,
Conceit and hypocrisy.”

“Even the king’s splendid chariot wears out,
So too this body eventually ages.
But the Dhamma of the good does not age —
So the good indeed speak to the good.”

“A person of little learning,
Like an ox, grows old;
His flesh increases,
But not his wisdom.”

title: “Monks, I will teach you about things that are bound and about binding. Listen. And what, monks, are the things that are bound? What is…
content: “Monks, I will teach you about things that are bound and about binding. Listen. And what, monks, are the things that are bound? What is binding? Form, monks, is a thing that is bound. Desire and lust for form is the binding of form. Feeling … Perception … Formations … Consciousness, monks, is a thing that is bound. Desire and lust for consciousness is the binding of consciousness. These, monks, are called the things that are bound. This is the binding.”

title: “And what, monks, is the practice of restraint? Here, monks, when a monk sees a form with the eye, he does not grasp the general…
content: “And what, monks, is the practice of restraint? Here, monks, when a monk sees a form with the eye, he does not grasp the general feature, does not grasp the particular feature. Those causes whereby, if the eye faculty were not restrained, covetousness, sorrow, evil, unwholesome states would arise — he restrains that cause, guards the eye faculty, practices restraint of the eye. When he hears a sound … smells an odor … tastes a flavor … feels a touch … cognizes a phenomenon with the mind, he does not grasp the general feature, does not grasp the particular feature. Those causes whereby, if the mind faculty were not restrained, covetousness, sorrow, evil, unwholesome states would arise — he restrains that cause, guards the mind faculty, practices restraint of the mind. This, monks, is called the practice of restraint.”

title: “One who has cut off craving, Without remainder, Dried up the swift stream — That monk abandons the near and far shore, Like a serpent…
content:
“One who has cut off craving,
Without remainder,
Dried up the swift stream —
That monk abandons the near and far shore,
Like a serpent sheds its worn-out skin.”

“One who has destroyed conceit,
Without remainder,
As a great flood destroys a fragile reed —
That monk abandons the near and far shore,
Like a serpent sheds its worn-out skin.”
